title: Lens Formula, Refraction of Light from Curved Surface

concepts.title: Refraction Formula through Single Spherical Surface
concepts.conceptBody:
Refraction from Curved Surfaces 

If the boundary between two transparent media is not plane and an object $\text{O}$ in medium of refractive index ${{\mu }_{1}}$ forms an image I in medium of refractive index ${{\mu }_{2}}$ as shown in Fig., then from Snell's law at the boundary AB

 

${{\mu }_{1}}\text{ sin i}={{\mu }_{2}}\text{ sin r}$

and as for small angles,

$\text{sin}\theta =\text{tan}\theta =\theta $

the above equation reduces to

$\begin{align} & {{\mu }_{1}}\quad \text{i}={{\mu }_{2}}\quad \mathbf{r} \\ & But\text{ }in \Delta {\mathrm O}CD \mathbf{i}=(\alpha +\beta ) \\ & while in \Delta \iota CD \beta =(\mathbf{r}+\delta ) \\ \end{align}$

So substituting the values of $\mathrm{i}$ and $\mathrm{r}$ from these in Eq. (1), we get

$\begin{align} & {{\mu }_{1}}(\alpha +\beta )={{\mu }_{2}}(\beta -\delta ) \\ & or {{\mu }_{1}}\alpha +{{\mu }_{2}}\delta =\left( {{\mu }_{2}}-{{\mu }_{1}} \right)\beta \\ \end{align}$

Or $\mu_1 \times \frac{\mathrm{y}}{(-\mu)}+\mu_2 \times \frac{\mathrm{y}}{(+\mathrm{v})}=\left(\mu_2-\mu_1\right) \times \frac{\mathrm{y}}{(+\mathrm{R})}$

Or $\frac{\mu_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{\mu_1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\left(\mu_2-\mu_1\right)}{\mathrm{R}}$

This is the desired result. If we compare it with mirror formula,

$\frac{1}{\text{v}}+\frac{1}{\text{u}}=\frac{1}{\text{f}}$

We find that here

$\text{v}\to \left( \text{v}/{{\mu }_{2}} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ u}\to \left( -\text{u}/{{\mu }_{1}} \right)$

So transverse magnification in this case

$\text{m}=\frac{\text{I}}{\text{O}}=-\frac{\left( \text{v}/{{\mu }_{2}} \right)}{\left( -\text{u}/{{\mu }_{1}} \right)}=\frac{{{\mu }_{1}}}{{{\mu }_{2}}}\cdot \frac{\text{v}}{\text{u}}$ …(3)

While using Eqs. (2) and (3) keep in mind that :

1.These are valid for all single refracting surfaces-convex, concave or plane. In case of plane refracting surface

$\text{R}\to \infty $, so Eq. (2) reduces to

$\frac{{{\mu }_{2}}}{\text{v}}-\frac{{{\mu }_{1}}}{\text{u}}=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{u}}{\text{v}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{1}}}{{{\mu }_{2}}}$

or $\frac{{{\text{d}}_{\text{AC}}}}{{{\text{d}}_{\text{AP}}}}=\frac{{{\mu }_{1}}}{{{\mu }_{2}}}$

2.The rules for signs for a single reflecting surface are same as for spherical mirrors.

3.If object or image itself is present at a refracting surface, refraction at that surface is not considered.

concepts.title: Thin Lenses
concepts.conceptBody:
Thin Lenses 

A lens is one of the most familiar optical devices for a human being. A lens is an optical system with two refracting surfaces. The simplest lens has two spherical surfaces close enough together that we can neglect the distance between them (the thickness of the lens). We call this a thin lens. 

Lenses are of two basic types convex which are thicker in the middle than at the edges and concave for which the reverse holds.

As there are two spherical surfaces, there are two centers of curvature ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ and correspondingly two radii of curvature ${{R}_{1}}$ and ${{R}_{2}}$.

The line joining ${{\text{C}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{C}}_{2}}$ is called the principal axis of the lens. The center $\text{P}$ of the thin lens which lies on the principal axis, is called the optical center .

Principal Focus : A lens has two surfaces and hence two focal points. First focal point is an object point on the principal axis for which image is formed at infinity

 

while second focal point is an image point on the principal axis for which object lies at infinity.

 

Focal length (f) : $\text{f}$ is defined as the distance between optical center of a lens and the point where the parallel beam of light converges or appears to converge.

Aperture : In reference to a lens, aperture means the effective diameter of its transmitting area. So the brightness, i.e., intensity of image formed by a lens which depends on the light passing through the lens will depend on the square of aperture, i.e.

$\text{I}\propto {{(\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Aperture }\!\!~\!\!\text{ })}^{2}}$

concepts.title: Sign Conventions for Lens
concepts.conceptBody:
Sign Conventions for Lens

1.Whenever and where ever possible, rays of light are taken to travel from left to right.

$\begin{matrix} {{\text{R}}_{1}}\left( =\text{O}{{\text{C}}_{1}} \right)=\text{ Positive } \\ \begin{align} & {{\text{R}}_{2}}\left( =\text{O}{{\text{C}}_{2}} \right)=\text{ Negative} \\ & \text{ f}\left( =\text{OF} \right)=\text{Positive} \\ & \text{ (A) } \\ \end{align} \\ \end{matrix}$    $\begin{align} & {{\text{R}}_{1}}\left( =\text{O}{{\text{C}}_{1}} \right)=Negative \\ & {{\text{R}}_{2}}\left( =\text{O}{{\text{C}}_{2}} \right)=Positive \\ & \text{f}\left( =\text{OF} \right)=Negative \\ & (B) \\ \end{align}$

2.Transverse distances measured above the principal axis are taken to be positive while those below it negative.

3.Longitudinal distances are measured from optical center are taken to be positive if in the direction of light propagation and negative if opposite to it, i.e., according to our convention in case of $\text{A}$ :

4.To calculate an unknown quantity the known quantities are substituted with sign in a given formula.

5.In the result sign must be interpreted as there are number of sign conventions and same sign has different meaning in different conventions.

concepts.title: Image Formation Rules for Lenses
concepts.conceptBody:
Rules for Image Formation 

In order to locate the image formed by a lens graphically following rules are adopted :

  1. A ray passing through optical center proceeds undeviated through the lens.
  2. A ray passing through first focus or directed towards it, after refraction from the lens, becomes parallel to the principal axis.
  3. A ray passing parallel to the principal axis after refraction through the lens passes or appears to pass through ${{\text{F}}_{2}}$.

Only two rays from the same point of an object are needed for image formation and the point where the rays after refraction through the lens intersect or appear to intersect is the image of the object. If they actually intersect each other the images is real and if they are near to intersect the image is said to be virtual.

concepts.title: Image Formed by Concave and Convex Lens
concepts.conceptBody:
Image Formed by Concave and Convex Lens 

1.For Convergent or Convex Lens 

2.For Divergent or Concave Lens

concepts.title: Lens Maker Formula
concepts.conceptBody:
Lens Maker Formula

In case of image formation by a lens, the incident ray is refracted twice, at first and second surface respectively. The image formed by the first surface acts as object for the second. So from the formula for refraction at curved surface.

 

$\frac{{{\mu }_{2}}}{\text{v}}-\frac{{{\mu }_{1}}}{\text{u}}=\frac{\left( {{\mu }_{2}}-{{\mu }_{1}} \right)}{\text{R}}$

For first surface

$\frac{{{\mu }_{\text{L}}}}{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}-\frac{{{\mu }_{\text{M}}}}{\text{u}}=\frac{\left( {{\mu }_{\text{L}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{M}}} \right)}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}$ …(1)

while for second surface as,

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \text{u}\to {{\text{v}}_{1}};{{\mu }_{1}}={{\mu }_{\text{L}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\mu }_{2}}={{\mu }_{\text{M}}} \\ \frac{{{\mu }_{\text{M}}}}{\text{v}}-\frac{{{\mu }_{\text{L}}}}{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}=\frac{\left( {{\mu }_{\text{M}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{L}}} \right)}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}} \\ \end{array}$ …(2)

So adding Eqs. (1) and (2), we have

${{\mu }_{\text{M}}}\left[ \frac{1}{\text{v}}-\frac{1}{\text{u}} \right]=\left( {{\mu }_{\text{L}}}-{{\mu }_{\text{M}}} \right)\left[ \frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}-\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}} \right]$

Or $\frac{1}{\text{v}}-\frac{1}{\text{u}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\left[ \frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}-\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}} \right]$

With $\mu =\frac{{{\mu }_{\text{L}}}}{{{\mu }_{\text{M}}}}$ …(3)

Now if object is at infinity, image will be formed at the focus, i.e., for $u=-\infty ,v=f$ so that above equation becomes

$\frac{1}{\text{f}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\left[ \frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}-\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}} \right]$ …(4)

which is known as Lens-maker's formula and in its light Eq. (3) for a lens becomes

$\frac{1}{\text{v}}-\frac{1}{\text{u}}=\frac{1}{\text{f}}$ …(5)

which is known as the Lens-formula. 

In case of lenses, transverse magnification is given by

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~\text{m}=\frac{\text{I}}{\text{O}}=\frac{\text{v}}{\text{u}} ...(6) \\ while power* & ~ \text{P}=\frac{1}{\left( \text{f }\!\!~\!\!\text{ in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m} \right)}\text{D } \\ {} & =\frac{100}{\left( \text{f }\!\!~\!\!\text{ in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ cm }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ dioptre }...\text{(7) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Some Important Points for Lenses
concepts.conceptBody:
Some Important Points for Lenses

1.For real extended objects if the image formed by a single lens is erect (i..e., $m$ is positive) it is always virtual. In this situation if the image is enlarged the lens is converging (i.e., convex) with object between focus and optical centre [Fig. (A)] and if diminished the lens is diverging (i.e., concave) with image between focus and optical center [Fig.(B)].

(A)

 (B)

2.For real extended object, if the image formed by a single lens is inverted (i.e., $m$ is negative) it is always real and the lens is convergent, i.e. convex. In this situation if the size of image is :

3.In case of inverted image formed by a lens the inversion is true, i.e., left is turned right and up, down as shown in Fig. (A).

(A)

(B)

4.As every part of a lens forms complete image, if a portion (say lower half) is obstructed (say covered with black paper) full image will be formed but brightness, i.e. intensity will be reduced (to half) [Fig. (B)].

5.If an object is moved at constant speed towards a convex lens from infinity to focus, the image will move slower in the beginning and faster later on, away from the lens. This is because in the time the object moves from infinity to $2\text{ F}$, the image will move from $\text{F}$ to $2\text{ F}$ and when the object moves from $2\text{ F}$ to $\text{F}$, the image will move from $2\text{ F}$ to infinity. At $2\text{ F}$ the speed of object and image will be equal. In case of lens, speed of image

${{\text{V}}_{\text{i}}}={{\text{V}}_{0}}{{\left[ \frac{\text{f}}{\text{u}+\text{f}} \right]}^{2}}$

where ${{\text{V}}_{0}}$ is the speed of object ( $\text{u}$ and $\text{f}$ are to be substituted with proper sign).

6.In case of sun-goggles, the radii of curvature of two surfaces are equal with center on same side, i.e.,

${{\text{R}}_{1}}={{\text{R}}_{2}}=\text{R}$

So $\frac{1}{\text{f}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\left( \frac{1}{\text{R}}-\frac{1}{\text{R}} \right)=0$

i.e., $\text{f}=\infty \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}=\left( 1/\text{f} \right)=0$

This is why sun-goggles have no power or infinite focal length. Same is true for a transparent sheet but with the difference that here ${{R}_{1}}={{R}_{2}}=\infty $.

7.If the two radii of curvatures of a thin lens are not equal, the focal length remains unchanged whether the light is incident on first face or the other. This is because if we substitute ${{R}_{1}}$ and ${{R}_{2}}$ with proper sign in lens-makers formula, we always have

(A)

(B)

$\frac{1}{\text{f}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\left[ \frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}} \right]$

8.If an equi-convex lens of focal length $\text{f}$ is cut into two equal parts by a horizontal plane $\text{AB}$ Fig. B], then as none of $\mu ,{{R}_{1}}$ and ${{R}_{2}}$ will change, the focal length of each part will be equal to that of initial lens, i.e.,

$\frac{1}{\text{f}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\left[ \frac{1}{\text{R}}-\frac{1}{\text{R}} \right]=\frac{2\left( \mu -1 \right)}{\text{R}}$

However in this situation as light transmitting area of each part becomes half of initial, so intensity will be reduced to half and aperture to $\left( 1/\sqrt{2} \right)$ times of its initial value [as $\text{I}\propto $ $\left. {{(\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Aperture }\!\!~\!\!\text{ })}^{2}} \right]$.

However, if the same lens is cut into two equal parts by a vertical plane CD [ Fig. (C)], the focal length of each part will become

$\frac{1}{{\text{{f}'}}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\left[ \frac{1}{\text{R}}-\frac{1}{\infty } \right]=\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)}{\text{R}}=\frac{1}{2\text{f}}$

i.e., ${f}'=2\text{f}$

i.e., focal length of each part will be double of initial value. In this situation as the light transmitting area of each part of lens remains equal to initial, intensity and aperture will not change.

 

9.If a lens is made of a number of layers of different refractive indices as shown, for a given wavelength of light will have as many focal lengths or will form as many images as there are $\mu $ 's as,

$\frac{1}{\text{f}}\propto \left( \mu -1 \right)$

10.As focal length of a lens depends on $\mu $, i.e., $\left( 1/\text{f} \right)\propto \left( \mu -1 \right)$, the focal length of a given lens is different for different wavelengths and maximum for red and minimum for violet whatever be the nature of lens.

(A)

(B)

11.If a lens of glass $\left( \mu =3/2 \right)$ is shifted from air $\left( \mu =1 \right)$ to water $\left( \mu =4/3 \right)$ then as :

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{A}}}}=\left[ \frac{3/2}{1}-1 \right]\text{K }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{W}}}}=\left[ \frac{\left( 3/2 \right)}{\left( 4/3 \right)}-1 \right]\text{K} \\ {} & \text{with }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}=\left[ \frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}-\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}} \right] \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \frac{{{\text{f}}_{\text{W}}}}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{A}}}}=\left[ \frac{8}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}} \right]\times \left[ \frac{\text{K}}{2} \right]\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{f}}_{\text{W}}}=4{{\text{f}}_{\text{A}}} \\ \end{array}$

i.e., focal length of a lens in water becomes 4 times of its value in air and so power one fourth $[$ as $\text{P}=1\left( 1/\text{f} \right)$.

12.If a lens is shifted from one medium to the other, depending on the refractive index of the lens and medium, the following three situations are possible :

(a)${{\mu }_{M}}<{{\mu }_{L}}$ but ${{\mu }_{M}}$ increases : In this situation $\mu =\left( {{\mu }_{L}}/{{\mu }_{M}} \right)$ will remain greater than unity but will decrease and as $\left( 1/f \right)\propto \left( \mu -1 \right),\left( 1/f \right)$ will decrease, i.e., f will increase (without change in nature of lens) as explained in point (11).

(b)${{\mu }_{M}}={{\mu }_{L}}$. In this situation $\mu =\left( {{\mu }_{L}}/{{\mu }_{M}} \right)=1$, so that $\left( 1/f \right)\propto \left( \mu -1 \right)=0$, i.e., $f=\infty $, lens will neither converge nor diverge but will behave as a plane glass plane

(c))${{\mu }_{M}}>{{\mu }_{L}}:$ In this situation $\mu =\left( {{\mu }_{L}}/{{\mu }_{M}} \right)<1$, so in lens-maker's formula sign of $f$ and hence nature of lens will change, i.e., a convergent lens will behave as divergent and vice-versa [Fig.].

concepts.title: Combination of Lens and Mirror
concepts.conceptBody:
Combination of Lens and Mirror

When several lenses or mirrors are used co-axially, the image formation is considered one after another in steps. The image formed by the lens facing the object serves as an object for the next lens or mirror, the image formed by the second lens (or mirror) acts as an object for the third, and so on. The total magnification in such situations will be given by

$\text{m}=\frac{\text{I}}{\text{O}}=\frac{{{\text{I}}_{1}}}{\text{O}}\times \frac{{{\text{I}}_{2}}}{{{\text{I}}_{1}}}\times \ldots $

i.e. $\text{m}={{\text{m}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\cdots $

In case of two thin lenses in contact if the first lens of focal length ${{f}_{1}}$ forms the image ${{I}_{1}}$ (of an object $\text{O}$ ) at a distance ${{\text{v}}_{1}}$ from it,

 

$\frac{1}{{{\text{v}}_{1}}}-\frac{1}{\text{u}}=\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{1}}}$ …(1)

Now the image ${{I}_{1}}$ will act as an object for second lens and if the second lens forms image $\text{I}$ at a distance $\text{v}$ from it, then

$\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{{{v}_{1}}}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{2}}}$ …(2)

So adding Eqs. (1) and (2), we have

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{f}_{2}}} \\ or & \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{F}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ with }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{F}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{f}_{2}}} \\ \end{array}$

i.e., the combination behaves as a single lens of equivalent focal length $\text{F}$ given by

$\frac{1}{F}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{f}_{2}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }P={{P}_{1}}+{{P}_{2}}$ ..(3)

Note : If the two thin lenses are separated by a distance $\text{d},\text{F}$ is given by

$\frac{1}{F}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{f}_{2}}}-\frac{d}{{{f}_{1}}{{f}_{2}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ so }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }P={{P}_{1}}+{{P}_{2}}-{{P}_{1}}{{P}_{2}}d$

Here it is worthy to note that :

1.If two thin lenses of equal focal length but of opposite nature (i.e., one convergent and other divergent) are put in contact, the resultant focal length of the combination will be

$\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}}=\frac{1}{\text{f}}+\frac{1}{-\text{f}}=0$

i.e., $\text{F}=\infty \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=0$

i.e., the system will behave as a plane glass plate.

2.If two thin lenses of same nature are put in contact, then as

$\begin{matrix} \frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}}=\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{1}}}+\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{2}}} \\ \frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}}>\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{1}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}}>\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{2}}} \\ \end{matrix}$

i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}<{{\text{f}}_{1}}$ and $\text{F}<{{\text{f}}_{2}}$

i.e., the resultant focal length will be lesser than smallest individual.

3.If two thin lenses of opposite nature with different focal lengths are put in contact, the resultant focal length will be of same nature as that of the lens of shorter focal length but its magnitude will be more than that of shorter focal length.

4.If a lens of focal length $\text{f}$ is divided into two equal parts as shown in Fig. (A) and each part has a focal length ${f}'$ then as

$\frac{1}{\text{f}}=\frac{1}{{\text{{f}'}}}+\frac{1}{{\text{{f}'}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {f}'}=2\text{f}$

 (A)

(B)

 

(C)

i.e., each part will have focal length $2\text{f}$.

Now if these parts are put in contacts as in Fig.(B) or (C) the resultant focal length of the combination will be

$\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}}=\frac{1}{2\text{f}}+\frac{1}{2\text{f}}\text{, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}=\text{f}\left( =\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ initial }\!\!~\!\!\text{ value }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)$

5.If a lens of focal length $\text{f}$ is cut in two equal parts as shown in Fig. (A), each part will have focal length $\text{f}$. Now if these parts are put in contact as shown in Fig. (B), the resultant focal length will be

(A)

(B)

 (C)

 

$\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}}=\frac{1}{\text{f}}+\frac{1}{\text{f}}\text{, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}=\left( \text{f}/2 \right)$

However if the two parts are put in contact as shown in Fig. (C), first part will behave as convergent lens of focal length $f$ while the other divergent of same focal length (being thinner near the axis); so in this situation

$\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}}=\frac{1}{\text{f}}+\frac{1}{-\text{f}}\text{, }\!\!~\!\!\text{ i}\text{.e}\text{., }\!\!~\!\!\text{ F}=\infty \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ P}=0$

concepts.title: Silvering of Lens
concepts.conceptBody:
Silvering of Lens 

If the back surface of a lens is silvered and an object is placed in front of it then :

 

1. First, light will pass through the lens and it will form the image ${{I}_{1}}$.

2. The image ${{I}_{1}}$ will act as an object for silvered surface which acts as curved mirror and forms an image ${{I}_{2}}$ of object ${{I}_{1}}$.

3. The light after reflection from silvered surface will again pass through the lens and lens will form final image ${{I}_{3}}$ of object ${{I}_{2}}$.

In such situation power of the silvered lens will be

$\text{P}={{\text{P}}_{\text{L}}}+{{\text{P}}_{\text{M}}}+{{\text{P}}_{\text{L}}}$

with $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{P}}_{\text{L}}}=\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{L}}}}$ where $\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{L}}}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\left[ \frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{1}}}-\frac{1}{{{\text{R}}_{2}}} \right]$

and ${{\text{P}}_{\text{M}}}=-\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{M}}}}$ where ${{\text{f}}_{\text{M}}}=\frac{{{\text{R}}_{2}}}{2}$

So the system will behave as a curved mirror of focal length $\text{F}$ given by

$\text{F}=-\frac{1}{\text{P}}$

To make this all clear we now consider the case of a silvered plano-convex lens under following circumstances.

1.When the plane surface is silvered and the object is in front of curved surface : 

In this situation

$\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{L}}}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\left[ \frac{1}{\text{R}}-\frac{1}{\infty } \right]=\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)}{\text{R}}$

And ${{\text{F}}_{\text{M}}}=\frac{\infty }{2}=\infty $

So ${{\text{P}}_{\text{L}}}=\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{L}}}}=\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)}{\text{R}}$

And ${{\text{P}}_{\text{M}}}=-\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{M}}}}=\frac{1}{\infty }=0$

and hence power of system

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & P={{P}_{L}}+{{P}_{M}}+{{P}_{L}}=2{{P}_{L}}+{{P}_{M}} \\ i.e., & P=2\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)}{R}+0=\frac{2\left( \mu -1 \right)}{R} ...(1) \\ \end{array}$

So $\text{F}=-\frac{1}{\text{P}}=-\frac{\text{R}}{2\left( \mu -1 \right)}$ …(2)

i.e., the lens will behave as a concave mirror of focal length $\left[ R/2\left( \mu -1 \right) \right]$.

(A)

 (B)

2.When the curved surface is silvered and the object is in front of plane surface : 

In this situation

$\frac{1}{{{\text{f}}_{\text{L}}}}=\left( \mu -1 \right)\left[ \frac{1}{\infty }-\frac{1}{-\text{R}} \right]=\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)}{\text{R}}$

And ${{\text{F}}_{\text{M}}}=\frac{\left( -\text{R} \right)}{2}$

So ${{P}_{L}}=\frac{1}{{{f}_{L}}}=\frac{\left( \mu -1 \right)}{R}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{P}_{M}}=-\frac{1}{{{f}_{M}}}=\frac{2}{R}$

and hence power of system

$\begin{align} & P={{P}_{L}}+{{P}_{M}}+{{P}_{L}}=2{{P}_{L}}+{{P}_{M}} \\ & i.e., P=\frac{2(\mu -1)}{R}+\frac{2}{R}=\frac{2\mu }{R} ...(3) \\ \end{align}$

So $\mathrm{F}=-\frac{1}{\mathrm{P}}=-\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2 \mu}$ …(4)

i.e., the lens will be equivalent to a converging mirror of focal length (R/2 $\mu )$.
